actions.label: Hire a Fractional COO
actions.description: Rent a part-time COO who picks and runs your smartest ops move each month, cutting your dependence on you.
actions.lesson: Key-person dependency is the silent killer of business value. You can't carry a full-time COO yet — so you rent the expertise part-time, start systemizing, and prove the role before you commit to the salary.
actions.narrative_success: Your fractional COO is steering operations a few days a month — picking the smartest systems-and-delivery move each turn. Part-time pay for now; a full-timer would build far harder.
actions.narrative_failure: The fractional COO is onboarding part-time. They'll be driving your ops moves from here.

actions.label: Promote COO to Full-Time
actions.description: Bring your COO full-time to build the machine daily, growing team and capacity while you step back. Pricey.
actions.lesson: A full-time A-player operator is what turns a business into a sellable asset — but they're expensive. You can only carry the payroll if your systems, margins, and leverage are doing the heavy lifting. Can't afford them? That's the ceiling telling you to fix your efficiency first.
actions.narrative_success: Your COO is full-time — and the machine is humming. Team and capacity grow on their own each month, and the business barely needs you. The salary is heavy, but your scale carries it.
actions.narrative_failure: Full-time offer signed; the bigger build is still ramping. Payroll bites from day one.

actions.label: Study Business Content (Books & YouTube)
actions.description: Learn the craft from books, YouTube, and podcasts. Free education that sharpens how you run and deliver.
actions.lesson: The cheapest leverage there is: a $20 book or a free video can save you months of expensive mistakes. Founders who keep learning out-execute those who wing it — invest your time before you can invest your money.
actions.narrative_success: A few late nights with the right books and channels, and ideas start clicking — you spot three things you've been doing the hard way and fix them.
actions.narrative_failure: You watched a lot and applied a little. Some of it will stick; consistency is the real lesson.

actions.label: Do the Work Yourself
actions.description: Deliver everything yourself to bank cash and master the craft. Unsustainable, but it gets invoices paid.
actions.lesson: Do it yourself first — not forever. You can't systematize or delegate what you don't deeply understand. Master the work, bank the cash, then escape it before you become the bottleneck.
actions.narrative_success: Everything got done. Everything. You're exhausted, but every client is happy — because you did it all personally, and the invoices got paid.
actions.narrative_failure: You dropped a ball. One client got a late delivery. Nothing catastrophic, but a reminder that one person can't do everything.

actions.label: Work With a Vendor Contractor
actions.description: Hand delivery to a contractor so you can focus on growing revenue instead of doing all the work yourself.
actions.lesson: Your first hire won't be as good as you — and that's fine. 80% done by someone else beats 100% done by you alone. But do the work yourself first: you can't hand off what you don't understand. Trading money for delivery capacity is the whole game.
actions.narrative_success: She's not as good as you. She's 80% as good, and that 80% just bought you back twenty hours a week of capacity.
actions.narrative_failure: The contractor needs more hand-holding than expected. You're spending time managing instead of doing — but that's the transition.

actions.label: Build a Delivery Team
actions.description: Hire two or three people dedicated to fulfillment. You manage, they deliver, and capacity scales past you.
actions.lesson: A dedicated delivery team is the leap from freelancer to business. The work happens whether you're in the room or not — that's a job becoming an asset with real capacity.
actions.narrative_success: The team handled this month's workload without a single escalation to you. You built something that works without you.
actions.narrative_failure: The team is hired and training. Quality isn't where you want it yet. You're managing more than doing — the awkward middle phase.

actions.label: Build Fulfillment System
actions.description: Document delivery end-to-end (intake, production, QA, follow-up) so you scale capacity without quality cracking.
actions.lesson: Turn delivery into an assembly line — intake, production, QA, delivery, follow-up, all documented. Consistency at scale lets you grow capacity without your quality cracking.
actions.narrative_success: A new client moves through your system like a product on an assembly line — consistent, reliable, scalable. This is a real business now.
actions.narrative_failure: The system is built for 80% of cases. The other 20% still need you. But that's 80% fewer fires.

actions.label: Build Proprietary IP
actions.description: Build proprietary tools or methodology rivals can't copy. Your moat, and your pricing power.
actions.lesson: A methodology competitors can't copy is a moat. Proprietary IP gives you pricing power and capacity to capture more value — not a commodity competing on price.
actions.narrative_success: The proprietary framework is built and branded. Clients now come to you for the methodology, not just the service.
actions.narrative_failure: The IP is developed but not yet differentiated enough. More iteration needed. Still, the foundation is laid.

actions.label: Expand Operations
actions.description: Bring a supplier in-house and clone your playbook into a new market to keep more margin and multiply reach.
actions.lesson: Once the playbook works, expand it — own more of the value chain to keep more of every dollar, and drop the proven system into a new market to multiply the business without reinventing it.
actions.narrative_success: You own more of the supply chain AND opened a second market — margins jumped and capacity multiplied. You're an empire now.
actions.narrative_failure: Expansion is messier than expected: integration overhead is real and the new market is slow. It'll take time to break even — if it does.

actions.label: Document & Automate Systems
actions.description: Document your process step by step and automate the repeatable parts so the work runs without you.
actions.lesson: Document before you delegate, then automate what repeats. SOPs move the process out of your head and onto paper; automation makes sure nothing slips through the cracks — together they're the first real step from operator to owner.
actions.narrative_success: What you do in your sleep now exists outside your head — written down and partly automated. Invoices go out on their own; scheduling is self-serve.
actions.narrative_failure: The SOP is written but incomplete and only some tasks are automated. You realize how much of your process is still instinct, not system.

actions.label: Implement Project Management
actions.description: Deploy real project tracking so everyone sees what's due, who owns it, and what's late. Nothing slips.
actions.lesson: Visibility is control. When everyone sees what's due, who owns it, and what's late, things stop falling through the cracks — and you stop being the human task-tracker.
actions.narrative_success: For the first time, you can see every active project, every deadline, every bottleneck — in one screen. This changes everything.
actions.narrative_failure: The tool is set up. Adoption is mixed. Your best contractor refuses to update her tasks. People problems, not software problems.

actions.label: Hire a Manager
actions.description: Add a manager so you oversee one person instead of five, buying back your time as the team grows.
actions.lesson: Manage one person instead of five. A management layer buys back your time and lets the team grow past your personal bandwidth — you stop being the ceiling.
actions.narrative_success: She ran the Monday meeting without you this week. The team didn't even notice you weren't there. That's the goal.
actions.narrative_failure: The new manager is learning. Team dynamics shifted. Two people aren't happy about the new layer. Growing pains.

actions.label: Full Systemization
actions.description: SOPs for everything, a manager on daily ops, and a business that runs without you. Owner, not operator.
actions.lesson: When the business runs without you, you've won the real prize: not just income, but freedom. You become the owner, not the operator — and your time becomes yours again.
actions.narrative_success: You took a week off. Revenue was the same when you came back. This is what freedom looks like.
actions.narrative_failure: Almost. Two client issues escalated to you during your 'vacation.' Close, but the safety net has holes.

actions.label: Hire Fractional CFO
actions.description: A part-time CFO who runs cash flow, debt, banking, and tax planning strategically instead of you winging it.
actions.lesson: Delegate the money function to someone who does it strategically — not on the side at midnight. Financial leadership is a force multiplier: better debt structure, lower taxes, smarter cash flow.
actions.narrative_success: Your CFO restructured two loan terms in her first month, found $3,000 in unnecessary expenses, and projected your cash flow for the next six months. You should have done this two years ago.
actions.narrative_failure: Onboarding is taking longer than expected — your books were messier than she anticipated. But she's cleaning them up.

actions.label: Hire an HR & Recruiting Lead
actions.description: Hire someone to own hiring, culture, and compliance so your team grows without you interviewing everyone.
actions.lesson: Past a handful of people, hiring becomes a job of its own. A people lead builds a repeatable pipeline so you recruit for fit (not desperation) and the team grows without you refereeing every issue.
actions.narrative_success: First hire she made on her own was better than your last three. The job posting, screening, and onboarding all happened while you were focused on strategy.
actions.narrative_failure: She's building processes. The org chart is getting formalized. It feels bureaucratic — but your team of 6+ needs it.

actions.label: Hire an Executive Assistant
actions.description: A right hand who handles credit utilization, your scores, and tax reserve every month so you never spend a turn on it.
actions.lesson: At some point your time is worth more than the admin. A great assistant owns the recurring financial housekeeping — utilization, credit, tax reserve — that quietly compounds in your favor while you focus on the big moves.
actions.narrative_success: Within a week your assistant had a spreadsheet of every account, a paydown schedule to keep utilization under 30%, the DUNS file current, and the tax reserve on autopilot. You stopped thinking about any of it — which is exactly the point.
actions.narrative_failure: Onboarding took longer than hoped, but the systems are in place now — the financial housekeeping runs itself from here.

actions.label: Build Quality Control
actions.description: Add a checklist and review so work goes out right the first time, even without you. Fewer mistakes, fewer churned clients.
actions.lesson: Quality has to survive your absence. A simple checklist keeps the work right when you're not the one doing it — and consistent quality is what keeps clients from leaving.
actions.narrative_success: Zero client complaints this month. The checklist caught two errors before they went out the door — and nobody churned.
actions.narrative_failure: The checklist exists. People are using it... sometimes. Consistency will take another month of enforcement.

actions.label: Build Client Onboarding
actions.description: Build a smooth intake with contracts, expectations, and kickoff. Nail the first week and clients stay longer.
actions.lesson: A great client experience is designed, not improvised. Nail the first week and you cut churn, earn referrals, and justify higher prices.
actions.narrative_success: New clients now get a welcome packet, a signed scope, and a kickoff call. You look like a real company now — and they stick around.
actions.narrative_failure: The onboarding flow is built but clunky. Half the clients still have questions the packet should have answered.

actions.label: Hire Client Success Manager
actions.description: Hire someone to own clients after the sale. Retention rises, churn falls, and you're no longer the only one they call.
actions.lesson: Keeping a client is cheaper than winning one. Delighted clients don't just stay — they hand you testimonials and referrals, the cheapest, highest-converting growth there is. Retention IS marketing.
actions.narrative_success: Three clients sent thank-you emails this month — none of them were to you. Your CSM is building relationships you used to own alone.
actions.narrative_failure: The CSM is learning your clients. Some awkward handoffs, but the clients appreciate having a dedicated contact.

actions.label: Build a Real Benefits Package
actions.description: Offer health, retirement match, real PTO, and leave. Benefits buy loyalty and turn a job into a career.
actions.lesson: Pay buys attendance; benefits buy loyalty. A real package lowers turnover, raises morale, and quietly heads off the people-drama that eats founders alive — for far less than the cost of replacing everyone who leaves.
actions.narrative_success: Open enrollment went out and the Slack lit up with thank-yous. People feel taken care of — and people who feel taken care of don't quit over a bad week.
actions.narrative_failure: The package is partway in place — health is live, retirement is still being set up. The team noticed the effort, at least.

actions.label: Grant Equity / Stock Incentives
actions.description: Grant vesting equity so key people own the upside and act like founders, not employees. Needs a C-Corp.
actions.lesson: Ownership is the ultimate retention and culture tool — people who share in the upside don't leave, don't coast, and don't create drama. Equity comp is why startups out-recruit big companies. It needs a C-Corp structure (an option pool, 409A valuation) to grant properly.
actions.narrative_success: You handed your key people their grant letters. The energy shifted overnight — they're not working for you anymore, they're building with you. Drama evaporates when everyone owns the outcome.
actions.narrative_failure: The option pool is set up but only partly granted — the 409A valuation took longer than expected. The intent landed, even if the paperwork lagged.
